speaker: interviewee
text: At this particular moment to-- to-- to, you know, remember, and-- and-- but I'm-- I'm trying. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you're doing a good job. I think what we'll do is we'll keep doing-- working on this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we'll do it. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the time we finish, your wife will be back from the beauty shop. We'll take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK. Or we can do it and she-- we can have lunch. Yeah, then she'll come back. You continue with my wife. You-- you-- you do whatever you have to do. OK. And-- and me and my wife we'll cooperate. We'll-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good. OK, good ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two for Daniel Geslewitz. And we were starting to talk about things getting dangerous in Poland. When do you remember-- do you remember when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when the war was about to start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we heard about Germany with Czechoslovakia. We heard about England negotiating with-- with-- with Germany and giving up half of Czechoslovakia. And we knew things are getting bad. And then Hitler marched into Czechoslovakia. And we knew about Austria, that Hitler marched into Austria. And the Austrians welcomed them with open arms. And we knew that next gonna be Poland. And we knew that it's gonna start over at the corridor, or someplace. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the corridor?

speaker: interviewee
text: The corridor is where Germany was kind of divided. Prussia, between Prussia and Germany, there was Poland. It was a corridor to-- to the sea, the Baltic. And the Germans demanded a corridor between Prussia and Germany. And we knew this gonna be the next pretext, Germany-- Germany to start the war. Or Silesia or part-- Silesia or something. They'll find something because we knew the Germans always find something, like they found with Sudeten Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Sudeten-- Deutch-- that's Sudetenland. That was Czechoslovakia. So we knew it's only-- it's a matter of time till we gonna have a war. And sure enough, the war started end of-- beginning of September, end of August. Were you back in school, yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was home.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this was vacation time. And I remember my brother was in the army. My other brother was mobilized. And me, being the youngest, I-- I was home with my sister. And we-- we-- we knew that the war was going to break out. But we didn't know exactly when. And sure enough, the war broke out. And everybody was participating-- expected something. And it didn't take long. It took about seven days. The German Army and the [? clutch ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and-- and things were no more the same. And-- and I remembered the German Army marching through Lodz. They all-- they were highly mobilized-- not mobilized-- mechanized.

speaker: interviewer
text: The army-- the German Army was highly mechanized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Highly mechanized. In those days, highly mechanized. You know, they were all rigid, you know, like those tanks, and trucks and trucks and trucks. They were-- a day they were marching. Because to Warsaw, they had to pass Lodz. There was no fights. The Pollack did not resist that. They gave up Lodz. So we didn't have any, you know, destruction. Only the Germans had plant up a couple bombs. That was about it. And when they entered, Lodz was intact besides a couple buildings that were damaged. And it started. You know, the-- as soon as they entered, they started to issue orders regarding the Jewish population.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: First they did not allow Jewish-- we could not assemble men, could not assemble more than seven men. You couldn't attend a-- you couldn't go to the synagogue. All the synagogues were closed. You could-- lots of things-- they couldn't do this, you couldn't go this. And if you had a factory, you couldn't operate it, and something like this. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they close your dad's shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we didn't close. We didn't have any work, because we didn't have anybody to sell it to. But in the beginning, it was like suspended. And then when they conquered Poland, they really started to issue orders against the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and then they started to take away businesses. In other words, if-- if a Jewish person had a business, a warehouse, they'll come, seal it, and-- and take it over. And they made the Jewish person responsible that everything is there. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the trucks will come and just empty the building without leaving a little piece of paper. They took it away. And then after it started, they suspected the Jews, they were hiding stuff. So they-- they had the Gestapo, used to take some of the Jews and used to beat them up mercilessly, that they should tell them where they hid some merchandise. And we knew-- I know we had one friend, and he had a leather business. And they took him into Gestapo. And they really worked him over. And he died a short time after. So in other words, it was terror all around. They-- you were not certain of anything. You-- you-- you couldn't walk out in the street. We tried not to walk in the city, if it wasn't necessary, because they would stop you and take you to work just to-- to intimidate you. And they could not distinguish between a Jewish person and a Polish person. As long as you didn't have a beard, you didn't dress with those black, you know, you didn't dress like a Orthodox Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would take a volunteer, a Polish kid, and they would point out-- they would know who's Jewish and who's not. They'll point out, this is-- you know, this is [? I ?] [? knew ?] [? that, ?] that's I knew that. And they will take him to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they'll take him to work to intimidate him. And they came back and beat them up and all kinds of stuff. So you didn't try to walk in the city if it wasn't necessary. So it was always that-- that danger, that frustration. You never knew what's going to happen next day, next minute. So we lived under that terror. We had no choice. And then on-- in November, I was waken up. And there was a fire, you know, we saw a fire the distance. Then they burned down all the synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. In November. We had the big synagogue, you know, beautiful synagogues. And a few more in the city. They burned down all the synagogues. And then they detonated it because the walls remained. So the Army came in and the demolition squad detonated it. The rubble was laying there. They would always have something up their sleeve to-- to intimidate us and-- and there was no let up. In 19-- in December, I would say, they issued an order that Lodz had to be made [? Judenreign ?] free of Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was December 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, January-- the end of-- December, January, something like that. I don't remember exactly. That Lodz should be [? Judenreign, ?] free of Jews. And all Jews had to leave Lodz. They didn't tell you where to go. And how about-- at that time, there was about close to 200,000 Jews in Lodz. To-- to move 200,000 Jews without transportation so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some Jews started to leave. They provided a train. They'll take a certain destination, from there, you're on you own. And Jews used to rent from farmers. You know, they used farmers and they used to pay them to-- to take them out of Lodz, in the middle of the winter. And the winter in Poland are real, real cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the process, lots of people froze to death. Children, mainly children. And the Germans had sat and talked about it. And they stopped it. But they-- they said that all the Jews will have to move in, and they will create a ghetto. And all the Jews will have to move into the ghetto, live in the ghetto. And in the process of moving all the Jews from uptown, like, I would say, and they always did it during the night, they will surround a few blocks. And everybody had to leave the building. And you can only carry one valise. In the process, in one night, 400 were shot because they were too slow. They did, you know, for any reason. And the Jews started to move in into the ghetto. They couldn't take any furniture with them. Only what they could carry. And you can see all the people carrying on their back with a mattress or whatever it is. And they moved into the ghetto. The ghetto was surrounded with barbed wire. And you couldn't get in and out of the ghetto. They-- they put a sentry every 100 yards. And April the 1st, if I'm not mistaken, they closed the ghetto. I mean, you could-- nobody could get in and nobody could get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before April 1st, they kept moving more and more Jews in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept on moving more and more, all the Jews from-- from the other part of Lodz, of the city of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the ghetto in relation to where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the ghetto-- we lived-- this was the ghetto. So the people living in the other parts of Lodz-- it was a big city-- moved into our part. But that was the most-- the poorest section of the city. There was no sewer, no running water. That was the poor section. And they squeezed in-- into the ghetto. When the ghetto closed-- if I'm not mistaken-- about 186,000 Jews in that small quarters. And in April, they closed the ghetto. I mean, closed-- anybody who was in the ghetto couldn't get out of the ghetto. They was outside couldn't get in unless, you know, he had to be transferred. No more Jews left in--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the area where they built the ghetto, were there non-Jews living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so were they taken out?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were moved out. They were ordered to move out. They could take the furniture. And our landlord was a German. And-- and-- before the war, we didn't know-- before the war, his son disappeared. Otto, his name was Otto. But when the war-- when the Germans entered, he came back dressed in a black uniform. In other words, they had prepared themselves. Even the Germans that lived in Poland, they prepared themselves, you know, for eventuality that Hitler will take over when they had crossed the border. So he came back. And-- and immediately, swastikas appeared in the window. The nice neighbor that we had no-- no quarrels with and we lived nice with them, and when the Germans moved in, entered Lodz, you could see in the windows they put swastikas.

speaker: interviewer
text: So many Volksdeutsche-- you never knew that they have so many Volksdeutsche. You know what the Volksdeutsche is? Why don't you explain it for the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Volksdeutsche is a Polish citizen of German descent. And when German-- the Germans entered Poland, they became Germans. And they were called the Volksdeutsche. And they were-- you know, they were-- there was no difference as far as they were behaving. They were even worse than the Germans in certain-- certain instances. So all the non-Jews had to move out of this area. It was strictly the Jewish-- a sort of-- a place for Jews to live. No other people lived in the ghetto. OK. And so this area-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --had to support 186,000 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- what do you remember especially about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it-- all of a sudden food became a problem. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's that? That was a problem, food, [BACKGROUND NOISE] because you couldn't get it. There was no food available. But some food was provided to the-- do you want-- you want me to see? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: So some food was provided by the German authority. And by the way, we had to pay for everything. We had to pay for the food. They didn't give us food. Even we're incarcerated, we had to pay for the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: With-- with goods or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to trade for the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly how, but we had to buy it. And then in order to sustain ourselves in the ghetto, we had to work. So we-- we established-- I say we, I'm talking the authorities-- established factories. See, the Germans selected certain Jews and said, you're gonna be the leader of the ghetto, which Rumkowski-- it happened to be his name was Rumkowski. And he and other people organized that and they established factories. We had cabinet making factories, shoe-making-- shoe-- shoe-making factories, we had tailor shops, everything. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of factory did you work in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- we used ot-- we used to call it [NON-ENGLISH]. We used to build--

speaker: interviewer
text: You better spell that one.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . It's very hard to spell it. It's-- let's me put it-- this was a woodworking shop, a woodworking factory. We use to build cribs, mainly, for-- cribs for Germany. We used to cut out--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean baby cribs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Baby cribs, to be taken out, taken to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: To be taken out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure, for-- for Germany. There was-- there was-- you see, there was a Jewish administration, and there was a German administration. The German administration used to take the goods that we manufactured, and they were selling them into Germany, to German, you know, businesses. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Somebody at the door. Can I take-- No. We can't stop for that. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We better stop. We can stop a minute. I'll take my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about how they-- how they did business with the factories that were established in the ghetto. You were talking about that you worked in a wood-making factory that made baby cribs. We made-- we made baby cribs, wooden shoes, and toys. The-- the-- the ghetto was run-- the-- by Rumkowski and a committee. Can you spell the Rumkowski for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-M-K-O-W-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So he was the Juden--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the Judenalteste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- you called Juden-- the head of the Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was like-- he wasn't elected. He was-- the Germans decided he-- he's gonna be the one. And he created like a-- you know, a committee or whatever and they run the business of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they connected in any way to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, absolutely. And then there were the Germans that had conducted the business from their side. They were buying-- They were taking the stuff that we manufactured, they were selling it to the businesses in Germany. And they made a hefty profit. It was a money-making business. They selled-- we manufactured uniforms, and they sell to the army, all kinds of stuff. And the German administration were making a good buck on it. And they were the providers of food. They were the-- they were the ones who decided how much food to provide, what kind of food. Because if they spent less money for the food, they were making more money. The bigger profit was for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German committee or the Judenrat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Judenrat-- only-- what food came in, the Judenrat was-- would try to divide the food as equally as possible. But they didn't have any say about providing the food, the-- what was coming in from outside the ghetto. This-- the German administration, they were the sole suppliers. They decided what to supply and how much and so forth and so on. Only the food that came into the ghetto. Then they knew how much food the Germans shipped in. And that was allotted so much-- let's say, two pounds of potatoes, or five pounds for two weeks, sometimes a can of little sugar or oil. And that was divided. You had those coupons. And they would say for this coupon, you get this and this and this and this or whatever. You used to go to the store, and they used to give it to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were stores, food stores, in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to buy. Only was-- they were given to you what they decided that everybody else should get. And now they decide five pound potatoes, you got five pound potatoes or beets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was more like a food warehouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: A food warehouse, a distribution.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not a store you can go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had coupons.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would get coupons. Coupon number two was [? whether ?] you get two potatoes, two pounds of potatoes, a half a pound of bread, and so on. This should have to last you for two weeks or a week, you know, something like that. And the administration tried to divide the food among the Jewish people living in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get more food because there were more children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. You didn't get any more food. Everybody got his card. [INAUDIBLE] They didn't give food for family. They gave food per person. And everybody had to manage his own food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children also? Well, you were pretty grown children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was pretty grown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had your own card.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had my own card, my own identification card. And I was getting so much. But before the war-- before the war, we used to eat collectively. And-- and after-- during the war, we couldn't eat collectively anymore. You couldn't put the bread on the table. So everybody had to manage his piece of bread should last him for-- for a week until you get the next piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was kind of-- it-- the family-- the family was no more, like sitting on the table and eating what mother would put on the table. It was everybody was getting his food had to manage by himself. So did you continue to eat together in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain things we used to eat together but somethings we ate separately. Like bread, we had to manage my own, how I ate my bread. You had to be very careful. Because if-- if-- if you got bread that should last you 10 days, you couldn't eat in one day because then what are you going to eat in nine days?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you have to be very, very careful-- self-control-- to eat the bread and divide it for nine or 10 days. And-- and eating this was a whole ceremony. You didn't eat it fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to-- you develop-- because we got in slowly-- you know, into that habit, you know, the behavior. And you ate the bread very slowly because you want to enjoy it for a longer period of time. And oh, you got potatoes or whatever. Anything was edible. We-- we would eat but not much was there, not much edible was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much stealing of food going on that you knew of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I mean, but from one person the other-- no, no. We-- we had very little crime in the ghetto, you know, like stealing from one person from the other. Everybody, you know, took care on his own. Maybe in some cases was-- was-- things were going on. But I'm not aware. There was no crime or murder for a piece of bread. There was none-- it was none-- was none-- never heard about it. You know, like one person kill the other person, take away the piece of bread. That was unheard of.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you went into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: 15. I was actually 15 and-- and 1/2. I wasn't 16 yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the ghetto for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, the whole family was still together.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the point, but then it stopped. My brother died in-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hersh.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he die of in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He got sick and took two days. The doctor came and-- and couldn't do anything for him. And then he just died. He-- he sort of-- I-- I don't know till this day what he died of, because no medication. There was-- there was-- it was-- so a doctor came. So what could he do for him? He didn't have any medication to give him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the '20s. And then, you know, my-- then my father died in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. He-- he died of hunger. There was a lot of hunger-- people dying in the ghetto. There was a lot of people die from hunger. There was a terrible hunger in-- in-- in the ghetto. And-- let's backtrack it. There was other things-- that-- there was hunger. There was cold. There were-- there were deportations.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure, all the time. All the time, deportations. As soon as they closed the ghetto, they started the departures. And then they used to-- and they-- they-- they started to deport always on the excuse for work. You know? They needed people to work there. There, the condition is going to be better, you know, and so forth and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people, they were hungry and they figure, what can be worse? They promised to give you food, better food, so you work. But actually, they-- those people never were heard of anymore. And-- So there was always something going on in the ghetto. And-- and they had in 19-- I guess-- 41, they said all the people that-- that could not find work, they're gonna be resettled. So all the families that-- that they couldn't find work, they have to-- they have to come to a certain place at a certain time, they were deported. Was that your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other families. We worked, we were craftsmen. You know, we were craftsmen, and we worked in the-- in the woodworking factories. But there were lots of people that couldn't find work. And they were supposed to be resettled. And the Germans told them to take tools with them, whatever they have, stoves, because they don't have it. And they went, and they were all exterminated. There were taken to Chelmno, I found out later, even after the war. Not far from Lodz was an extermination camp, Chelmno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-E-L-M-N-O. That was a-- a strictly extermination camp. And they used to gas them in trucks. They-- they tried to-- in the different ways, you know, to-- to kill. And they took those people to Chelmno. And the clothes used to come back to the ghetto to be sorted. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they brought in the German Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought German Jews into Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Into the ghetto. They brought Jews from Czechoslovakia. Because you see, the population got less, you know, due to hunger and starvation, deportation. So they made-- Lodz was like a holding pen. You know-- they-- if they needed Jews to a certain amount to be exterminated, they didn't have to go to look for them. They would come to the ghetto and say to them, we need 10,000 Jews, or something like this. And you provide for-- for resettlement. So they didn't have to go, you know, look around for the 10,000 Jews, little villages here and there. They were right there. So Lodz was like a holding pen for the machinery, for the-- for the destructive machinery that was going on. They brought in Jews from-- from-- from Germany, from Czechoslovakia, from Austria. And those Jews, when they came, they lived as free until they got to the ghetto. But they got to the ghetto, all of a sudden, when it hit them, the conditions, they hardly could take it because that was-- they-- they lived a normal-- you know, they had food. When they arrived, they had lived in Germany, they came to the ghetto, we lived already on a starvation diet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying that the Polish Jews in the ghetto were more adjusted than the other Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were more adjusted. Because we-- we slowly got into that way of life. When they came in, and they didn't know what to-- where-- they came into ghetto, and they, all of a sudden, they were confronted with a different way of life. And-- and that was a shock to them. And it was very hard on them. And lots of them-- they just-- just-- lots of them died because they couldn't live on that little bit of food that they were getting. Where we, we knew already the little soup. We knew how to manage it. But they were not used to that. And then they were held there in this group. There were taken out and taken away and not to be seen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the clothes came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothes-- lots of clothes came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how you knew that people were being murdered?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we saw-- we-- we suspected that-- that something is going on. You see, we didn't have the luxury to confront the truth. Because we were in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had become so hopeless-- helpless. This way, you-- you kidded yourself. We-- part of survival was to kid yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got it. All right. We're gonna stop right now. We're at the end of the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, how is it going?

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, wait, wait. Wait till David turns it off, OK?
